claim: Trail cameras provide behavioral intelligence — confirming whether a lost dog will enter a trap, what time it visits a feeding station, and from which direction it approaches and departs.
stance_target: Camera trap deployment strategy for lost dog movement corridor mapping
scope_conditions: Lost pet recovery using feeding stations and humane traps
quoted_support: They can help provide evidence that the lost pet is in the area and whether s/he will enter a humane trap if one is set up.

claim: Standard deer-hunting camera height of 24–36 inches is too high for detecting small dogs and cats; cameras must be positioned lower to capture the animal reliably.
stance_target: Camera trap setup parameters for lost dog detection
scope_conditions: Trail cameras deployed for lost pet recovery near feeding stations
quoted_support: Since their website is aimed primarily at deer hunters, the height that they suggest placing the camera (24"-36") is too high for cats and small dogs, but most of the information is applicable to setting up a camera to locate missing pets.

claim: Camera must be positioned far enough back from the food or bait that both the entry and exit direction of the animal are visible in the frame — not just the animal at the bait.
stance_target: Camera trap field positioning to capture movement corridor intelligence
scope_conditions: Camera deployment at lost dog feeding stations
quoted_support: Try to get the camera far enough back from the food so that you can determine which way your dog is entering the scene and which way he is leaving.

claim: The Browning Dark Ops Elite trail camera is recommended for lost pet work because its adaptive flash adjusts strength based on animal proximity, avoiding image whiteout that loses identity confirmation.
stance_target: Camera hardware selection for Nona lost dog monitoring infrastructure
scope_conditions: Wildlife trail camera selection for lost pet recovery
quoted_support: My current favorite camera is the Browning Dark Ops Elite. The Browning has a feature that adjusts the strength of the flash based on how close the animal is to the camera. A too bright flash that whites out the animal in the photo is a common problem with many wildlife cameras.

claim: Using surveillance cameras (trail cameras, motion alarms, baby monitors) drastically increases success rate of locating and catching lost pets when combined with feeding stations or traps.
stance_target: Camera trap + feeding station combination as a core Nona search tactic
quoted_support: Using some method of surveillance can drastically increase the success of locating and catching your missing pet when using feeding stations and/or trapping.
